_meta.tz_note_he: All times are in local time unless otherwise noted.
_meta.method_he: Solar/lunar eclipses, moon phases, supermoons, oppositions, Mercury/Venus elongations and equinoxes/solstices were computed in Skyfield (JPL DE440s ephemeris) for Alex's exact coordinates. Meteor-shower data (ZHR, peak time, radiant) from the IMO Meteor Shower Calendar 2026; the moon's illumination percentage on the peak night was computed for the site. Eclipse circumstances (coverage percentage, the sun/moon's altitude above the horizon) were computed pointwise for Larkmont — what he will *really* see from the yard, not the worldwide values.
sources: IMO 2026 Meteor Shower Calendar (imo.net) — ZHR, peak solar-longitude, radiants
NASA GSFC eclipse catalogue / timeanddate.com (cross-check of eclipse types & global paths)

events.detail_he: A strong shower (ZHR≈120, radiant in Boötes), but in 2026 the moon is nearly full (≈99% illuminated) — the light washes out almost everything and leaves only the bright meteors. A sharp, brief peak around 22:00–02:00. A weak year for this shower.

events.title_he: Jupiter at opposition — gas giant all night
events.detail_he: Jupiter opposite the sun, rising at sunset and setting at sunrise — as bright as it gets this year (distance 4.23 AU). Declination +22° → very high in the sky over Larkmont, ideal for a telescope: the four Galilean moons and the cloud belts are visible even with good binoculars. The night's high point is around midnight.
events.best_time: All night, high in the south at midnight

events.detail_he: Mercury at its greatest angular distance from the sun (≈18°) — the best viewing is after sunset, low above the western horizon at twilight. Brief and not easy; the wider elongation in the east (pre-dawn) on April 4 will be much better.
events.best_time: Right after sunset, low western horizon

events.title_he: Total lunar eclipse — but not visible from the area
events.detail_he: A global total lunar eclipse, but its peak is at 13:33 our midday, when the moon is 46° *below* the horizon (broad daylight). It's seen well from the Asia–Pacific region; from Larkmont — not at all. Recorded here for accuracy.
The sun crosses the celestial equator (18:45). Day and night are nearly equal; from here the nights grow shorter, but the Milky Way core begins to climb into the pre-dawn sky.

events.title_he: Opening of Milky Way core season
events.detail_he: From April through September the galactic core (Sagittarius) sits in the dark sky. In April it rises only before dawn; as summer progresses it rises earlier. At Alex's latitude the core climbs to ≈30° above the southern horizon — and under Larkmont's Bortle 3 skies you can see it clearly with the naked eye.
events.best_time: Before dawn in spring, late at night in summer; south

events.title_he: Mercury at western elongation (morning) — the best of the year
events.detail_he: The best chance to see Mercury in 2026: an elongation of ≈27.8° west of the sun, visible low in the morning sky in the east before sunrise. Larkmont's dark skies help catch it through the twilight.
events.best_time: About an hour before sunrise, eastern horizon

events.detail_he: A small but long-established shower (ZHR≈18, radiant in Lyra). In 2026 the moon is only ≈38% illuminated and sets early — reasonable conditions after midnight. The Milky Way is already rising in the east later in the night.

Debris from Halley's Comet, a fine shower for the southern hemisphere but also visible from these latitudes before dawn in the east (ZHR≈50, fast meteors). In 2026 the moon is ≈84% illuminated — a less favorable year; wait for 2027 (no moon).
events.best_time: One to two hours before sunrise, east

events.detail_he: Mercury ≈24.5° east of the sun — visible after sunset low in the west. One of the better evening apparitions of the year.

events.title_he: Summer solstice — the longest day of the year
events.detail_he: The sun at its northernmost point (11:24), the longest day and shortest night. From here the nights begin to lengthen again — toward August's long Milky Way nights.

Summer's beloved shower at its peak (ZHR≈100, bright meteors from the direction of Perseus in the northeast). And in 2026 the conditions are perfect: new moon on August 11 → completely dark skies all night. Under Larkmont's Bortle 3 skies this could be one of the finest meteor nights of the decade. Sit outside after midnight, give your eyes 20 minutes to adjust.
events.best_time: From 23:00 until dawn; northeast and up

events.title_he: Total solar eclipse (Europe) — not visible from Larkmont
events.detail_he: A total solar eclipse whose path of totality crosses Iceland and northern Spain. Many will write that it's 'partially visible from the area' — but the calculation for Alex's coordinates shows that the partial eclipse only begins *after* the sun has set at Larkmont (sunset ≈19:25), so the coverage percentage while the sun is above the horizon is 0%. In other words: from Larkmont it isn't actually visible. Recorded honestly.

events.title_he: Venus at eastern elongation — 'the Evening Star'
events.detail_he: Venus at its greatest distance east of the sun (≈46°) — the brightest point in the western evening sky after sunset. Very prominent to the naked eye; through binoculars you can see it's at half phase (like a half moon).
events.best_time: After sunset, high in the west

events.title_he: Partial lunar eclipse — barely, low below the horizon
events.detail_he: A partial eclipse that's seen well globally, but from Larkmont its peak (07:12 in the morning) occurs when the moon is already ≈12° below the horizon (after it has set) and the sun has already risen. In practice not visible from the yard.
The sun crosses back southward (03:05). The nights lengthen; the Milky Way core is still visible early in the evening in the southwest — the end of core season.

Neptune opposite the sun — but at magnitude 7.8 it requires binoculars or a telescope (as a faint bluish point). Distance 28.9 AU. Declination ≈0° → on the celestial equator, rising high enough from Larkmont.

events.title_he: Saturn at opposition — a night of rings
events.detail_he: Saturn opposite the sun, visible all night (distance 8.43 AU). In 2026–2027 the rings are barely open toward us (almost 'edge-on') — a rare and actually interesting situation. A small telescope already reveals the disk and the thin ring.
events.best_time: All night; telescope required for the rings

events.detail_he: Mercury ≈25.2° east of the sun — a convenient evening to catch it low in the west after sunset.
Debris from Halley's Comet (ZHR≈20, fast). In 2026 the moon is ≈80% illuminated — a moderate-to-weak year. The radiant is in Orion, high after midnight.
A fast, bright shower (ZHR≈15 in a normal year, radiant in Leo). In 2026 the moon is ≈55% illuminated — moderate conditions; the radiant rises after midnight.

Uranus opposite the sun, at magnitude ≈5.6 — at the limit of naked-eye visibility at a dark site like Larkmont, and a clear greenish point through binoculars. Declination +20.7° → high in the sky, good conditions.

events.title_he: Geminids peak — king of the meteor showers
events.detail_he: The richest shower of the year (ZHR≈150, slow, colorful meteors from the direction of Gemini). In 2026 the moon is only ≈20% illuminated and rises late — very fine conditions. Cold at night, but the Bortle 3 skies are worth it: dozens of meteors per hour are possible.
events.best_time: From 21:00 until dawn; whole sky, peak at midnight

events.title_he: Winter solstice — the longest night of the year
events.detail_he: The sun at its southernmost point (22:50), the shortest day and longest night — the good time for winter skies: Orion, Taurus, Canis Major and all the bright stars dominate the sky.

events.title_he: Supermoon (full) — the closest of the year
events.detail_he: A full moon at a distance of 356,738 km — the closest and largest of 2026 (slightly larger and brighter than usual). Lovely to the eye and for photography above the valley's ridgeline, but it washes out the sky — not a night to hunt for faint objects.
After the poor 2026, in 2027 the moon is only ≈13% illuminated — excellent conditions for this strong shower (ZHR≈120). A sharp, brief peak, around midnight to 02:00, northeastern radiant.

events.title_he: Venus at western elongation — 'the Morning Star'
events.detail_he: Venus ≈47° west of the sun — shining in the eastern morning sky before sunrise. The brightest point in the sky after the moon.
events.best_time: Before sunrise, high in the east

events.detail_he: Jupiter again opposite the sun, all night (4.36 AU, declination +15°). High and bright; the Galilean moons through binoculars.

events.title_he: Mars at opposition — the Red Planet close by
events.detail_he: Mars opposite the sun and at a small distance (0.68 AU) — bright and strikingly orange, visible all night. With a telescope you can try to see ice caps and dark patches. Declination +15.5° → high and convenient from Larkmont.

events.title_he: Penumbral lunar eclipse — visible but subtle
events.detail_he: A penumbral eclipse: the moon is very high (≈62°) at peak time (01:12) and is seen well from Larkmont — but the darkening is very subtle, only a slight shading at the moon's edge. Not dramatic, but real.

events.title_he: Mercury at western elongation (morning) — the best of 2027
events.detail_he: Mercury ≈27.6° west of the sun — the year's best chance to see it, in the morning sky in the east before sunrise.
events.best_time: About an hour before sunrise, east

events.detail_he: The sun crosses the celestial equator (22:24). The start of Milky Way core season before dawn.

events.title_he: Eta Aquariids peak — an excellent year
events.detail_he: In 2027 the moon is new (≈0% illuminated) — perfect conditions for Halley's Comet debris (ZHR≈50, fast). The radiant is in Aquarius, low but visible before dawn in the east from the dark skies.

The 'long eclipse of the century': the path of totality (up to 6 min 22 sec near Luxor, Egypt) passes just south of the area. From Larkmont you see a *deep* partial eclipse — maximum coverage ≈85.4% of the sun (magnitude 0.871) at peak ≈13:02, when the sun is very high (≈77°). The partial eclipse lasts from ≈11:41 to ≈14:21. This is the decade's great sun-moon event for him! Eclipse glasses / a solar filter are mandatory — never look at the sun directly. The light will turn strange and ghostly at peak.
In 2027 the moon is ≈84% illuminated on peak night — washing out many of the meteors. Still worth going out after midnight once the moon is low, to catch the bright ones. ZHR≈100.

events.title_he: Venus at eastern elongation — 'the Evening Star'
events.detail_he: Venus ≈45.8° east of the sun, glowing in the western evening sky after sunset.
The sun crosses southward (09:01); the nights lengthen.
Saturn opposite the sun all night (8.33 AU). The rings are still very narrow toward us — an interesting situation; in 2027 they begin to open again. Telescope recommended.
Uranus opposite the sun, magnitude ≈5.6, declination +21.5° → high. A greenish point through binoculars at the dark site.

events.title_he: Geminids peak — but a full moon
events.detail_he: King of the meteor showers (ZHR≈150), but in 2027 the moon is full (≈100%) exactly on peak night — washing out most of the meteors. Only the bright ones will survive. A weak year for the Geminids.
The longest night of the year (04:42); winter skies full of bright stars.

A partial eclipse that's only partly visible from Larkmont: at peak (06:13 in the morning) the moon is very low (≈6° above the western horizon, moments before it sets). You can catch its beginning if the western horizon is open — and from Larkmont, above the valley, it is open.
events.best_time: Before dawn, very low in the west

events.detail_he: The sun crosses the equator (04:17).

events.title_he: Venus at eastern elongation — 'the Evening Star'
events.detail_he: Venus ≈46° east of the sun, shining in the evening in the west.
In 2028 the moon is nearly new (≈3% illuminated) — wonderful conditions for the Lyrids (ZHR≈18). Radiant in Lyra, visible all night.
The longest day of the year (23:02). The nights begin to lengthen toward Milky Way season.

events.title_he: Partial lunar eclipse — visible from Larkmont
events.detail_he: A partial lunar eclipse in which, at peak (21:19), the moon is ≈16° above the eastern horizon — low but clearly visible from the yard. Earth's shadow bites into part of the moon. A pleasant evening for viewing early in the night.

events.title_he: Saturn at opposition — the rings open
events.detail_he: Saturn opposite the sun all night (8.23 AU). The rings open up more and look lovely in a small telescope. Declination +11° → convenient from Larkmont.
The sun crosses southward (14:45).
In 2028 the moon is only ≈19% illuminated — good conditions for the fast Orionids (ZHR≈20, Halley debris). Radiant in Orion, high after midnight.
In 2028 the moon is only ≈3% illuminated — dark skies for the fast, bright Leonids. Worth getting up before dawn, radiant in Leo in the east.

In 2028 the moon is only ≈6% illuminated — ideal conditions for the king of the meteor showers (ZHR≈150). The combination of a rich Geminids + Bortle 3 skies + a nearly new moon = one of the best meteor nights of the decade. Bundle up, bring a thermos of tea, and sit beneath the dome.
The longest night of the year (10:19).

events.title_he: Total lunar eclipse on New Year's Eve — visible! 🌕
events.detail_he: A *total* lunar eclipse seen well from Larkmont: at peak (18:52) the moon is ≈25° above the eastern horizon, in a dark evening sky. The moon turns copper-red (a 'blood moon') when it's fully in Earth's shadow — and all of this on New Year's Eve. A spectacular naked-eye event; the dark skies will intensify the color.
The sun crosses the equator (10:01).

events.title_he: Mars at opposition — the closest of the decade
events.detail_he: Mars opposite the sun at a very small distance (0.65 AU) — bright and strikingly orange, visible all night. The closest opposition of this five-year span; an excellent telescope opportunity (ice caps, surface details). Declination ≈+1° → on the equator, convenient.
In 2029 the moon is ≈44% illuminated — moderate conditions; the radiant in Aquarius is low, visible before dawn in the east. ZHR≈50.
The longest day of the year (04:48).

events.title_he: Total lunar eclipse — but the moon has already set
events.detail_he: A global total lunar eclipse, but from Larkmont at peak (06:22 in the morning) the moon is already ≈8° below the horizon and the sun has risen — in practice not visible from the yard. The Pacific/Americas regions see it. Recorded for accuracy.
In 2029 the moon is only ≈11% illuminated — nearly dark skies for the beloved Perseids (ZHR≈100). A pleasant summer night, meteors from the northeast.
The sun crosses southward (20:38).

events.title_he: Venus at eastern elongation — 'the Evening Star'
events.detail_he: Venus ≈47° east of the sun — shining very brightly in the western evening sky.
Saturn opposite the sun all night (8.14 AU), the rings nicely open (declination +16°, high). Telescope recommended.
In 2029 the moon is ≈62% illuminated on peak night — moderate conditions; once the moon is low you can still catch many bright meteors (ZHR≈150).

events.title_he: Total lunar eclipse near zenith — the eclipse of the decade 🌕
events.detail_he: A *total* lunar eclipse seen almost perfectly from Larkmont: at peak (00:42 at night) the moon is ≈74° above the horizon — nearly overhead! The moon entirely in Earth's shadow, turning deep copper-red, high in the dark Bortle 3 skies. This is the best lunar eclipse from Larkmont in the entire five-year span — don't miss it.
events.best_time: Around midnight, high near the zenith

events.detail_he: The longest night of the year (16:14).
In 2030 the moon is new (≈0% illuminated) — ideal conditions for the strong shower (ZHR≈120). A sharp, brief peak, northeastern radiant. Completely dark skies at Larkmont.
The sun crosses the equator (15:52).
Jupiter opposite the sun all night (4.39 AU). Declination -17° → lower in the south from Larkmont, but still bright and impressive through binoculars.
events.best_time: All night, high in the south at midnight

events.title_he: Annular solar eclipse (Asia) — 60% coverage from Larkmont 🌒
events.detail_he: An annular solar eclipse ('ring of fire') whose path crosses Europe and Asia. From Larkmont you see a fine partial eclipse — maximum coverage ≈60.3% of the sun (magnitude 0.694) in the morning, at peak ≈07:49, when the sun is low-to-moderate (≈26° above the eastern horizon). A nice morning event — but eclipse glasses / a solar filter are mandatory, never look directly.

events.title_he: Partial lunar eclipse — visible from Larkmont
events.detail_he: A partial lunar eclipse in which, at peak (21:33), the moon is ≈19° above the southeastern horizon — clearly visible from the yard in the evening. Earth's shadow bites into part of the moon.
The longest day of the year (10:31).

events.title_he: Perseids peak — but a full moon
events.detail_he: In 2030 the moon is full (≈100%) on peak night — washing out most of the meteors. Only the bright ones will survive. A weak year for the Perseids. ZHR≈100.
The sun crosses southward (02:26).
In 2030 the moon is only ≈27% illuminated — good conditions for the fast Orionids (ZHR≈20). Radiant in Orion, high after midnight.
In 2030 the moon is ≈54% illuminated — moderate conditions for the fast Leonids. Radiant in Leo, rising after midnight.

events.title_he: Geminids peak — a waxing moon interferes
events.detail_he: King of the meteor showers (ZHR≈150), but in 2030 the moon is ≈86% illuminated on peak night — washing out a lot. Only the bright ones. A better window early in the evening before the moon is high.
events.best_time: Early evening before the moon rises high

events.detail_he: The longest night of the year (22:09); the end of the five-year span under bright winter skies.
